Homework 2

Frank Marino

Load Libraries

# Load any R Packages you may need
rm(list = ls())
library(mosaic)
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

Load Data

The following exercises use the airbnb_boston.csv data set. Download the .csv file from our Canvas course and load the file into R to complete this assignment. Call this data airbnb_boston.

airbnb_boston <- read.csv("airbnb_boston.csv", header = TRUE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

Exercise 1

(a)

Use favstats() to find several descriptive statistics for price and minimum_nights, separated by neighbourhood.

# favstats() for price
fav_price <- favstats(~ price | neighbourhood, data = airbnb_boston)
# favstats() for minimum_nights
fav_min_night <- favstats(~ minimum_nights | neighbourhood, data = airbnb_boston)

(b)

Which neighborhood has the highest average required price?

high_avg <- which.max(fav_price$mean)
highest_avg <- fav_price$neighbourhood[high_avg]

The neighbourhood that has the highest average required price is South Boston Waterfront.

Which neighborhood has the lowest average required price?

low_avg <- which.min(fav_price$mean)
lowest_avg <- fav_price$neighbourhood[low_avg]

The neighbourhood that has the lowest average required price is Mattapan.

(c)

Which neighborhood has the highest average required minimum_nights?

min_night_avg <- which.max(fav_min_night$mean)
min_night_neigh <- fav_min_night$neighbourhood[min_night_avg]

The neighbourhood that has the highest average required minimum_nights is Leather District.

Which neighborhood has the lowest average required minimum_nights?

min_night_avg_low <- which.min(fav_min_night$mean)
min_night_neigh_low <- fav_min_night$neighbourhood[min_night_avg_low]

The neighbourhood that has the lowest average required minimum_nights is Bay Village.

colors <- ifelse(fav_price$mean == max(fav_price$mean), 
                 "brown3",
                 ifelse(fav_price$mean == min(fav_price$mean),
                        "darkgreen",
                        "grey"))
fav_price <- fav_price %>%
  mutate(
    price_level = case_when(
      mean == max(mean) ~ "Highest average price",
      mean == min(mean) ~ "Lowest average price",
      TRUE ~ "Other neighborhoods"
    )
  )
ggplot(fav_price, aes(x = reorder(neighbourhood, mean), y = mean)) +
  geom_col(fill = colors) +
  coord_flip() +
  expand_limits(y = max(fav_price$mean) * 1.1) + 
  labs(
    title = "Average Airbnb Price by Neighborhood",
    x = "Neighborhood",
    y = "Average Price Per Night (USD)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
   geom_text(
    data = fav_price %>% 
      filter(price_level != "Other neighborhoods"),
    aes(label = paste0("$", round(mean, 0))),
    hjust = -0.1,
    size = 3.5
  )

min_night <- fav_min_night %>%
  mutate(
    night_level = case_when(
      mean == max(mean) ~ "Highest average min nights",
      mean == min(mean) ~ "Lowest average min nights",
      TRUE ~ "Other neighborhoods"
    )
  )

ggplot(min_night,
       aes(x = mean, y = reorder(neighbourhood, mean))) +
  
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = 0, xend = mean, yend = neighbourhood),
    color = "grey70"
  ) +
  
  geom_point(
    aes(color = night_level),
    size = 3
  ) +
  
  geom_text(
    data = min_night %>% 
      filter(night_level != "Other neighborhoods"),
    aes(label = round(mean, 1)),
    hjust = -0.55,
    size = 5
  ) +
  
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "Highest average min nights" = "brown3",
      "Lowest average min nights" = "darkgreen",
      "Other neighborhoods" = "grey60"
    )
  ) +
  
  expand_limits(x = max(min_night$mean) * 1.05) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Average Minimum Nights Required by Neighborhood",
    x = "Average Minimum Nights",
    y = "Neighborhood",
    color = "Category"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

combined <- bind_rows(
  fav_price %>%
    transmute(neighbourhood, value = mean, metric = "Average Price (USD)"),
  fav_min_night %>%
    transmute(neighbourhood, value = mean, metric = "Average Minimum Nights")
)
ggplot(combined,
       aes(x = value, y = reorder(neighbourhood, value))) +
  
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = value, yend = neighbourhood),
               color = "grey70") +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "steelblue") +
  
  facet_wrap(~ metric, scales = "free_x") +
  
  labs(
    title = "Neighborhood Comparison of Airbnb Pricing and Stay Requirements",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Neighborhood"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Exercise 2

Boston has a lot of great neighborhoods to visit. Let’s compare your top three neighborhoods. Using the code chunk below, filter the airbnb_boston data set to three of the neighborhoods and call this filtered data set airbnb_boston_filtered:

  • Allston
  • Back Bay
  • Bay Village
  • Beacon Hill
  • Brighton
  • Charlestown
  • Chinatown
  • Dorchester
  • Downtown
  • East Boston
  • Fenway
  • Hyde Park
  • Jamaica Plain
  • Leather District
  • Longwood Medical Area
  • Mattapan
  • Mission Hill
  • North End
  • Roslindale
  • Roxbury
  • South Boston
  • South Boston Waterfront
  • South End
  • West End
  • West Roxbury

Filter Data

NOTE: Be sure to match exactly the spelling and case sensitivity when selecting categories or your data set might not filter properly!

# Type your code here 
airbnb_boston_filtered <- airbnb_boston %>%
  filter(neighbourhood %in% c("Beacon Hill", "South Boston Waterfront", "Dorchester"))

Examine the filtered data set by performing R analysis to compare the three neighborhoods by creating one chart/graph that helps compare your neighborhoods.

You may provide any other analysis that you think would enhance your visualization or aid users in understanding your data. You may compare your neighborhoods using any of the variables available in the data set:

  • price
  • reviews_per_month
  • accommodates
  • bathrooms
  • bedrooms
  • beds
  • property_type
  • room_type
  • neighbourhood
  • minimum_nights

Not sure what kind of graph you want to create? Try looking in the R Data Visualization Cookbook for some great “recipes” to try out.

R Analysis

Perform all your R analysis here. Use comments to break-out the different elements you are going to include.

airbnb_entire <- airbnb_boston_filtered %>%
  filter(room_type == "Entire home/apt")

ggplot(airbnb_entire,
       aes(x = neighbourhood, y = accommodates, fill = neighbourhood, )) +
  geom_violin(trim = FALSE, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.1, outlier.shape = NA, fill = "white", 
               aes(color = "Median")) +
stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", size = 3,
               aes(color = "Mean")) +
    scale_color_manual(
    name = "Statistic",
    values = c("Median" = "black", "Mean" = "black")
  ) +
scale_fill_manual(values = c(
  "Beacon Hill" = "darkgreen",   # soft orange
  "Dorchester" = "brown1", # soft blue
  "South Boston Waterfront" = "deepskyblue4" # soft red/pink
))+ 
  theme_minimal() +
  guides(fill = "none") +
  labs(
  title = "Distribution of Airbnb Sizes by Neighborhood in Boston",
  x = "Neighborhood",
  y = "Accomodation (Number of People)"
) +
  theme(
    legend.position = c(0.65, 0.75),  # adjust x, y (0-1) as needed
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = alpha("white", 1), color = "black")
  )

Exercise 3

(a)

Create a Canva Presentation with two slides:

  • Slide 1: Create a data visualization for the data in Exercise 1, placing special emphasis on comparing the different neighborhoods.

  • Slide 2: Create a data visualization for the data in Exercise 2, placing special emphasis on the chart/graph you created to compare your three selected neighborhoods.

(b)

Embed your Canva Presentation Here:

Distribution of Airbnb Prices by Neighborhood in Boston by Frank Marino

INSERT IFRAME EMBED CODE. DON’T FORGET to add in data-external="1" at the end of your <iframe> or else R-Markdown will not display properly.

(c)

Trouble embedding? No worries! Provide your Canva link here as a back-up
